item.title: Archeopteryx Tile
item.description: I loved visiting the Natural History Museum as a child. The excitement of entering that main hall, where the diplodocus skeleton filled the space. And there was a replica of this fossil, perhaps the most famous of them all.

item.title: Buffalo Woodcut
item.description:
This was carved from a piece of plywood using Flexcut and Pfeil tools. I used the vintage Columbian press at the Swansea Print workshop. What a privilege to use a machine with such history, and still in perfect working order.

I’ve printed it on kitakata and Wenzhou paper. I am drawn to the delicate but strong texture of the Wenzhou paper. It’s translucent, as you can see in the video.

item.title: Canada Geese Tetrapak
item.description:
I often sketch these geese down by the Pub on Pond. Once they’ve realised I have no food for them they get on with preening and nibbling the grass.

This tetrapak plate has been torn on the upper edge and I applied masking tape to the geese bodies. I aligned the birds’ toes along the creases of the opened tetrapak.

item.title: Crab and Seaweed Cyanotype
item.description:
I love beachcombing. I have a collection of seaweed, mermaids purses and so on, which get washed, dried, pressed ready for use.

I combined these found materials with one of my sketches, printed on acetate and laid on the paper along with the seaweed and mermaids purse.

I was disappointed by this image when it first appeared. The materials cannot have been flat against the glass in the lightbox, leading to the blurred result. But now I like it for exactly that effect, as if you’re peering into a rockpool.

item.title: Crab and Seaweed Cyanotype 2
item.description: A crisper image than first time round, but the blue isn’t quite as intense.

item.title: Swimming Crab and Seaweed Mandala
item.description:
I’m lucky to live by the sea. When the tide is low I’ll head out with my brother to see what we can find.

Most animals hide away and wait for us to pass (although curiosity will often tempt a lobster out). Not so the velvet swimming crabs. These ninja crustaceans are on the attack! They move at speed and can reach behind their backs with their pincers.

This piece is my very first mandala print. It has a rotational symmetry of 6, which makes construction easier: use a compass set to the side length of the tile.

I cut the block from softcut using essdee tools. The paper is Japanese washi and I use Cranfield Caligo inks along with a studio roller and baren.

item.title: Crab Tetrapak
item.description:
My first tetrapak print, done at a workshop in Penarth run by Sarah Hannis.

It’s based on sketches I made of an edible crab which I bought at Swansea market.

item.title: Eel Tile
item.description: This is inspired by a young conger eel my brother found when we were rockpooling at Oxwich on a very low tide.

item.title: Gwen Seated
item.description: Always a privilege to draw Gwen.

item.title: John Twice
item.description: Always a pleasure to draw John. Such a presence. These are short poses from an Elysium life drawing session.

item.title: King Vulture Tile
item.description: This is based on a sketch I made of the King Vulture specimen in the Bristol Museum. I carved it using my printmaking tools.

item.title: Seaweed Cyanotype
item.description:
I love beachcombing. I have a collection of seaweed, mermaids purses and so on, which get washed, dried, pressed ready for use.

Something about cyanotypes reminds me of the sea. The blue, maybe, and the way the image fades in when you wash the paper, like waves ebbing away

item.title: Taffy Tile
item.description:
Taffy is a springer spaniel who loves going on a walk but also takes her rest and relaxation seriously.

This tile is based on sketches I made of Taffy when looked after her for a few days.

item.title: Toad and Dandelion Mandala
item.description:
I’m always happy to see a toad in the garden. They bring luck and eat slugs. Dandelions are often thought of as weeds, but they are cheery and robust, and you can tell what time it is by blowing the seed heads.

The design has a rotational symmetry of 6. 6 is a perfect number, being the sum of its proper divisors 1, 2 and 3. Each finished print will have 1, 2, 3 or 6 seeds applied using a hand-made stamp

I cut the block from Japanese vinyl using Flexcut tools. The paper is Japanese washi and I use Cranfield Caligo inks. The process is documented in my instagram post.

item.title: Toad Tile
item.description:
Toads bring luck and I am always delighted to find one in the garden. When we lived in Reynoldston there was one which made a home in the coal shed, digging itself down into the dusty floor. And back in Bristol a toad patrolled the elephants ears by the garden wall.

You can see me making this one in my instagram reel.

item.title: Alan
Ana Standing
Carys Standing
item.description: One of the amazing models at the Elysium life drawing sessions.

item.title: Gwynfor Seated
item.description: This one is from a session at the Swansea Print Workshop.

item.title: Lloyd and Leo
item.description: Queer Shapes modeling collective sometimes work in pairs. These are quick poses at the start of an Elysium life drawing session: too quick to get both figures down. But Leo is there, a fading yellow shape between the standing Lloyds

item.title: Tonya Seated
Tonya Standing
